questions.prompt: Why does Tesla co-locate suppliers at the Gigafactory campus?

choices.text: Lower logistics cost (heavy/bulky parts cost a lot to ship far), reduced inventory (JIT works better at minute-scale than day-scale), faster engineering velocity (supplier engineers in the next building can iterate same-day instead of weeks of remote back-and-forth), better quality coordination, and supply continuity (less exposure to shipping disruptions). The combination is the operational dividend.

questions.explanation: Co-location compresses logistics, inventory, engineering iteration, and quality coordination — multiple dividends from physical proximity. The downside is concentration risk and lock-in, which Tesla manages with multi-site strategy and selective vertical integration.

questions.prompt: Should commodity fasteners (basic screws, bolts) be co-located with the Gigafactory?

choices.text: No. Commodities don't justify the costs of co-location (reduced supplier competition, real estate commitments, lock-in). Reserve co-location for: high-volume parts where logistics dominates (cells, seats); items with engineering iteration where supplier collaboration matters; strategic suppliers where lock-in is worth the dependency. Commodities should stay competitive: many suppliers, lowest bid wins, ship from wherever.
Only if Tesla also makes screws.

questions.explanation: Co-location is expensive. It pays back on heavy/bulky parts, on parts requiring engineering iteration, on supply-continuity-critical inputs. Commodities don't fit — keep them competitive and ship from wherever is cheapest.
